label: Western Front — Initial Stabilization (Nov 1914)
notes: Approximate line after First Battle of Ypres. Western Front ran from Swiss border to English Channel, ~700 km. Largely static for four years.

label: Schlieffen Plan — German Advance on Paris (Aug–Sep 1914)
notes: German First Army swept through Belgium and northern France, reaching within 30 miles of Paris before halted at the Marne River.

label: German Retreat After First Marne (Sep 1914)
notes: German forces retreated approximately 65 km from the Marne to the Aisne River after their defeat in the First Battle of the Marne.

label: Great Retreat — BEF from Mons (Aug 1914)
notes: British Expeditionary Force retreated 250 miles in 13 days from Mons to the outskirts of Paris, fighting rear-guard actions to cover the French retreat.

label: German Advance Through Belgium (Aug 1914)
notes: Germany violated Belgian neutrality, bypassing and reducing fortress cities of Liège and Namur before sweeping southward toward France.

label: Eastern Front — Initial 1914 Position
notes: Eastern Front after initial German victories at Tannenberg and Masurian Lakes. Far more fluid than the Western Front due to vast distances.

label: Great Retreat — Russia Pushed Back (1915)
notes: German and Austro-Hungarian forces advanced 300–500 km during the 1915 Great Retreat, capturing Warsaw and all of Congress Poland.

label: Brusilov Offensive — Russian Advance (Jun–Sep 1916)
notes: Russian forces advanced up to 160 km on a 300-mile front, the most successful Allied offensive of the war. 400,000 prisoners captured.

label: Allied Naval Approach to Gallipoli (Feb–Mar 1915)
notes: Allied fleets assembled at Mudros Bay on Lemnos and approached the Dardanelles for the naval campaign before the land landings.

label: RMS Lusitania Final Voyage
notes: Lusitania sailed from New York to Liverpool, torpedoed by U-20 off Old Head of Kinsale, Ireland, killing 1,198 of 1,962 aboard.

label: Italian-Austrian Front — Isonzo River
notes: Italian-Austrian front line along the Isonzo River, scene of eleven Italian offensives 1915–1917. Italy advanced only a few kilometers at enormous cost.

label: Caporetto — Austro-German Advance (Oct–Nov 1917)
notes: After the breakthrough at Caporetto, Italian armies fled 150 km to the Piave River, losing 275,000 prisoners and 2,500 guns.

label: Serbian Army's Winter Retreat Through Albania (1915–16)
notes: 155,000 Serbian troops and civilians retreated through Albania in winter conditions after Bulgarian entry into the war, suffering catastrophic losses.

label: Arab Revolt — Hejaz Railway Raids
notes: T.E. Lawrence and Arab irregulars systematically sabotaged the Hejaz Railway (Medina to Damascus), disrupting Ottoman supply lines throughout 1916–1918.

label: Allenby's Palestine Campaign (1917)
notes: British forces advanced from Egypt through Gaza, Beersheba, and Jaffa to capture Jerusalem in December 1917, then continued to Damascus in 1918.

label: Macedonian Front (1915–1918)
notes: Allied armies in Salonika held a front in Macedonia from 1915. The September 1918 offensive broke through Bulgarian lines, collapsing the Central Powers' southern flank.

label: Grand Fleet Sortie to Jutland (May 31, 1916)
notes: British Grand Fleet (151 warships) sailed from Scapa Flow to intercept the German High Seas Fleet in the North Sea off Denmark.

label: U-boat War Zone — North Atlantic/British Isles
notes: Germany declared the waters around Britain a war zone in February 1915, later intensifying to unrestricted submarine warfare. U-boats sank 5,000+ Allied ships.

label: Somme Offensive — Maximum Allied Advance (Jul–Nov 1916)
notes: Despite 1.2 million casualties, the Somme offensive advanced only 10 km over 141 days. Germany withdrew to the Hindenburg Line in early 1917.

label: German Advance at Verdun (Feb–Aug 1916)
notes: German advance at Verdun peaked at 9 km before French counterattacks in October–December 1916 recovered most of the lost ground.

label: Hindenburg Line — German Withdrawal (Feb–Mar 1917)
notes: Germany withdrew 40–65 km to the Hindenburg Line (Operation Alberich), straightening and shortening their defensive line and devastating the abandoned territory.

label: Operation Michael — German Spring Advance (Mar–Apr 1918)
notes: Germany's Spring Offensive advanced up to 65 km—the largest Western Front gain since 1914—but failed to achieve strategic objectives and exhausted elite stormtroop units.

label: Hundred Days Offensive — Allied Advance (Aug–Nov 1918)
notes: Allies advanced steadily from Amiens to the German border in 100 days, employing combined-arms tactics. The greatest Allied military achievement of the war.

label: Romania Invades Transylvania (Aug 1916)
notes: Romania declared war on Austria-Hungary and invaded Transylvania with 350,000 men. Initial gains were reversed when Germany and Bulgaria counterattacked, occupying Bucharest by December.

label: Ottoman-Russian Caucasus Front
notes: Ottoman Army launched winter offensive in the Caucasus in December 1914, defeated at Sarikamish with 90,000 casualties—the catalyst for blaming Armenians and initiating the genocide.

label: Zeppelin Raids on Britain (1915–1918)
notes: Germany conducted 51 Zeppelin raids on Britain, dropping 5,806 bombs and killing 557 civilians. The raids caused widespread panic but were increasingly countered by RFC fighters.

label: Canadian Corps Advance — 100 Days (Aug–Nov 1918)
notes: Canadian Corps spearheaded the Hundred Days Offensive, advancing over 130 km and capturing 31,537 prisoners and 623 guns—the most successful Canadian military operation.

label: ANZAC Corps Advance — 100 Days (Aug–Nov 1918)
notes: Australian Corps (under Monash) and New Zealand Division advanced steadily, distinguished by meticulous combined-arms planning and execution.

label: AEF Meuse-Argonne Advance (Sep–Nov 1918)
notes: 1.2 million American soldiers attacked in the Meuse-Argonne, America's largest battle. Targeted critical Sedan–Mézières railway, cutting off German supply lines.

label: Gorlice-Tarnów Breakthrough (May–Sep 1915)
notes: German-Austro concentrated artillery broke Russian lines at Gorlice, advancing 160 km in three months and occupying all of Congress Poland by September 1915.

label: Allied Convoy System — North Atlantic (1917–1918)
notes: Britain introduced the convoy system in May 1917 to counter U-boat warfare. Monthly Allied shipping losses fell from 860,000 tons (April 1917) to 289,000 tons (November 1917).

label: German High Seas Fleet — Bottled in North Sea
notes: After Jutland, Germany's High Seas Fleet never again challenged British naval dominance, remaining largely in port for the remainder of the war.

label: Nivelle Offensive — Chemin des Dames (Apr 1917)
notes: Nivelle promised a decisive breakthrough within 48 hours. The offensive failed after minimal advances, triggering mutinies in 54 French divisions.

label: French Counteroffensives at Verdun (Oct–Dec 1916)
notes: French forces under Mangin recaptured Fort Douaumont (Oct 24) and Fort Vaux (Nov 2), then pushed German lines back nearly to February starting positions by December.

label: Combined Arms Advance — Hamel to Amiens (1918)
notes: Monash's Battle of Hamel (July 4) demonstrated coordinated tank-infantry-artillery-aircraft tactics that became the template for the August Amiens offensive.

label: Operation Georgette — Attack on Ypres (Apr 1918)
notes: Germany's second Spring Offensive aimed to seize the Channel ports. Initial gains caused Haig to issue his 'backs to the wall' order. The offensive petered out without decisive gains.

label: Second Marne — Allied Counteroffensive (Jul–Aug 1918)
notes: French and American forces counterattacked on July 18, exploiting the failure of Germany's last offensive. The turning point that shifted initiative permanently to the Allies.

label: First Chlorine Gas Attack — Langemarck to Ypres (Apr 22, 1915)
notes: 168 tonnes of chlorine gas released from 5,730 cylinders drifted on the wind toward Allied lines. Breached a 4-mile gap before Canadian troops stemmed the advance.

label: American Troops Arrive in France (Jun 1917 onwards)
notes: First US Army troops landed at Saint-Nazaire on June 26, 1917. By November 1918, over 2 million AEF soldiers were in France, providing the decisive numerical advantage.

label: Russian Caucasus Advance — Erzurum (Jan–Feb 1916)
notes: Russia's Caucasus Army under Yudenich captured the fortress of Erzurum in February 1916 and Trebizond in April, the most significant Russian victory of the war.

label: Czech Legion on Western Front (1918)
notes: Czech and Slovak volunteers formed the Czechoslovak Army Corps and fought on the Western Front and Salonika fronts, establishing their nation's claim to independence at Versailles.

label: Mines Detonated Under Messines Ridge (Jun 7, 1917)
notes: 19 mines containing 455 tonnes of explosive were detonated simultaneously under Messines Ridge, creating an explosion heard in London 210 km away. 10,000 Germans killed instantly.

label: Breaking the Hindenburg Line (Sep–Oct 1918)
notes: British, Australian, and American forces breached the Hindenburg Line using canal tunnels as approach routes, collapsing Germany's main defensive system.

label: Salonika Offensive — Bulgaria Collapses (Sep 1918)
notes: Franco-Serb-Greek forces broke through Bulgarian lines on September 15, 1918, forcing Bulgaria's armistice on September 29—the first crack in the Central Powers' collapse.

label: Tank Corps Mass Advance — Cambrai (Nov 20, 1917)
notes: 476 British tanks advanced en masse, achieving a 7-km breakthrough on the first day. Church bells rang in London prematurely. A German counterattack recovered most gains.

label: Third Ypres (Passchendaele) Advance (Jul–Nov 1917)
notes: British forces advanced only 8 km through seas of mud over 100 days. 310,000 British and 260,000 German casualties. The village of Passchendaele taken November 6.

label: British 'Pals Battalions' — Recruitment Drives (1914–15)
notes: Kitchener's New Army recruited 1 million volunteers by December 1914, organized into 'Pals Battalions' of men from same towns. Many entire communities wiped out on July 1, 1916.

label: Battle of Vimy Ridge — Canadian Advance (Apr 9, 1917)
notes: All four Canadian divisions attacked simultaneously for the first time, capturing Vimy Ridge in one day using rehearsed combined-arms tactics. Canada's defining national battle.

label: Voie Sacrée — Verdun Supply Route
notes: France maintained Verdun's supply through a single road—the Voie Sacrée—running 75 km to Bar-le-Duc. 6,000 trucks made constant round trips, carrying 25 million men in 1916.

label: SMS Goeben & Breslau — Entry into Black Sea (Aug 1914)
notes: German warships Goeben and Breslau evaded British pursuit and entered the Dardanelles, leading to their transfer to the Ottoman Navy and drawing the Ottomans into the war.

label: Ottoman/German Raid on Russian Black Sea Ports (Oct 29, 1914)
notes: Ottoman warships (actually German-crewed) bombarded Sevastopol, Odessa, and Novorossiysk, drawing the Ottoman Empire into the war and prompting Russia, Britain, and France to declare war.

label: Arab Forces Advance to Aqaba (1917)
notes: T.E. Lawrence and Auda abu Tayi led Arab irregulars on a 600-mile desert march to attack Aqaba from the land side, capturing it on July 6, 1917.

label: AEF Advance — Argonne Forest (Sep–Nov 1918)
notes: American forces battled through the dense Argonne Forest, one of the most difficult terrain features of the war, cutting German supply routes and forcing the Armistice.

label: US Troop Convoys to France (1917–1918)
notes: US transported 2 million soldiers to France virtually without loss to U-boat attack, thanks to the convoy system and anti-submarine escorts, proving Allied mastery of the Atlantic.

label: Operation Blücher — Third Aisne Attack (May 27, 1918)
notes: Germany's surprise attack at Chemin des Dames advanced 60 km in three days, reaching the Marne—threatening Paris. But the offensive overextended German supply lines.

label: Mangin's Counterattack — Villers-Cotterêts (Jul 18, 1918)
notes: General Mangin's Tenth Army struck from Villers-Cotterêts forest with 346 tanks and no warning bombardment, catching Germany completely by surprise—the start of the Allied final offensive.

label: Ottoman Attack on Suez Canal (Feb 1915)
notes: Ottoman forces under Cemal Pasha attempted to seize the Suez Canal, threatening British communications with India and Australia. The attack was repulsed by British and Indian defenders.

label: Allied Evacuation of Gallipoli (Dec 1915–Jan 1916)
notes: The most successful Allied operation of Gallipoli—250,000 troops evacuated with almost no casualties. The only major Allied success of the entire campaign.

label: Belgian Army Holds the Yser (Oct 1914)
notes: Belgian forces opened sluice gates at Nieuport, flooding the Yser River plain and halting the German advance on the Channel coast—holding Belgium's last unoccupied territory.

label: Russian Great Retreat (May–September 1915)
notes: Following the Gorlice breakthrough, Russian armies retreated up to 500 km, losing 2 million men and all of Congress Poland. Russia abandoned offensive strategy for the rest of 1915.
